justification: All pass criteria met: 9 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria (well beyond the 3 minimum), covering happy path (AC-1), already-shipped/in-transit (AC-3), already-cancelled/terminal states (AC-4), plus authorization (AC-5), concurrency (AC-7, AC-8), and atomicity (AC-6). Multiple ambiguities surfaced in both confidence.low_confidence_areas (7 items) and escalations (4 detailed escalation items) covering cancellable states, fee structure, recipient notification, and return logistics. confidence.level is 40, well below the 65 threshold. Business rules like fee amounts and cancellation windows are explicitly flagged as assumptions or escalated as ambiguities rather than presented as facts. edge_cases section is richly populated with 9 scenarios.

observations.text: 9 acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum 3, covering happy path, multiple edge cases, authorization, concurrency, and atomicity
confidence.level of 40 correctly reflects the underspecified input — well below the 65 threshold
4 detailed escalations with options and recommendations surface key ambiguities: cancellable states, fee structure, recipient notification, return logistics
7 low_confidence_areas explicitly flag unknowns including who can cancel, fee amounts, refund policy, and recipient notification
Business rules like cancellation fees (BR-3, BR-4) are marked invariant:false with clear justification that they are policy decisions, not facts
Edge cases section is thorough with 9 scenarios including race conditions, idempotency, batch orders, and promotional codes

BR-2 asserts 'only the order owner may cancel' as an invariant, which is arguably an invented rule — operations staff or support agents often cancel on behalf of users. While flagged in low_confidence_areas indirectly, it could be more explicitly called out as an assumption
The escalation on cancellable states explicitly offers three options (A/B/C) without dictating the answer, correctly treating it as a product decision

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 8 Given/When/Then criteria covering happy path (AC-1, AC-2), delivered edge case (AC-3), already-cancelled edge case (AC-4), plus authorization (AC-5), courier notification (AC-6), concurrency (AC-7), and out-for-delivery (AC-8). At least 6 ambiguities surfaced in low_confidence_areas and escalations, including cancellable statuses, cancellation fees, refund policy, who can cancel, courier compensation, and partial order cancellation. Confidence level is 42, well below the 65 threshold. Edge cases section is thoroughly populated. Assumptions are explicitly labeled as assumptions, not presented as facts.

observations.text: 8 acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum 3, covering happy path, multiple edge cases, authorization, and concurrency
Confidence level of 42 appropriately reflects the underspecified single-sentence input
6 low_confidence_areas explicitly surfaced, including the key ambiguities: cancellable statuses, who can cancel, fee structure, refund policy
4 escalations with structured options and recommendations — does not invent answers, presents choices for stakeholders
Assumptions section clearly labels inferred rules (e.g., 'out for delivery is too late to cancel — but this is an assumption, not confirmed')
Edge cases section is comprehensive with 8 scenarios including concurrency, payment, partial fulfillment, and special goods

Some business rules (BR-1 'only the order owner may cancel', BR-4 cancellation fees, AC-8 rejecting out-for-delivery) are stated fairly assertively in the rules section despite being flagged as assumptions in the confidence section — the dual framing could cause downstream agents to treat them as confirmed requirements
Defines 'Cancellation Window' and 'Order Owner' in ubiquitous language with specificity not present in the original spec, though these are later acknowledged as assumptions

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria (far exceeding the 3 minimum), covering happy path (AC-1/2/3), already-shipped rejection (AC-4), already-cancelled idempotency (AC-6), authorization (AC-5), race conditions (AC-7/8/9), and audit (AC-10). 6 low_confidence_areas surfaced plus 5 detailed escalations covering cancellation window, authorized actors, refund policy, receiver notification, and multi-parcel ambiguity. Confidence level is 35, well below the 65 threshold. Business rules are clearly labeled as assumptions, not presented as facts. Edge cases section is thoroughly populated with 8 scenarios.

observations.text: 10 acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum 3, covering happy path, multiple edge cases, and race conditions
Confidence level of 35 appropriately reflects the single-sentence underspecified input
6 explicit low_confidence_areas including cancellation window boundaries, who can cancel, cancellation fees, multi-parcel handling, receiver notification, and void vs refund
5 structured escalations with options and recommendations — none presented as settled facts
Assumptions are clearly labeled in a dedicated assumptions array, not smuggled in as business rules
BR-2 explicitly marks sender-only cancellation as invariant:false with justification that it's a business policy assumption
Edge cases section covers 8 scenarios including race conditions, concurrent requests, payment provider failures, and partial fulfillment
Domain analysis correctly identifies the spec as 'dangerously underspecified' upfront

BR-1 defines specific cancellation window states (PLACED, CONFIRMED, ASSIGNED) and marks it as invariant:true, which is slightly overconfident for an assumed boundary — though the testable_assertion uses a clearly non-cancellable state (IN_TRANSIT)
Idempotency for already-cancelled orders is handled in both AC-6 and BR-7 with clear reasoning
